entry.title: Unlocking the Practical Value of AI for Procurement: Autonomous Spend Management
entry.content:
Introduction

Over the years, Procurement has radically transformed from being “manual” to “digitised”, and now it has entered the exciting age of Artificial Intelligence (“AI”). There has never been an age with more powerful tools or greater opportunities for Procurement. However, there are some misconceptions about AI which need to be cleared up. This situation is not helped by the view that it is easy to simply put AI in the title of anything to make it sound relevant and/or interesting. Fortunately, this view is not universally shared. The reality is that AI is the only topic in the room whichever way you turn as the next-generation experience.

Recent research shows that large-scale digital transformation programs continue to fail most often because of organisational and operational issues rather than technology limitations. Common causes include poor governance, weak stakeholder alignment and unrealistic program scoping. At the same time, there is increasing pressure on organisations to demonstrate quick and measurable ROI from the investment in transformation programs. These challenges and issues also apply to AI.

AI is rapidly reshaping Procurement, but many teams are still asking the same question: “What really works in practice?”

 

Practical Value of AI for Procurement

Many use cases, underpinned by proven examples, emphasise the role of AI in enhancing productivity and operational efficiency by automating repetitive tasks such as purchase order creation, low-value RFx, and settling payment disputes. CPOs and their procurement teams can focus more on strategic activities including category strategy, supplier collaboration, and risk management. Further to this, AI brings capacity to tail spend management which falls below the threshold for Central Procurement teams for more effective control and driving cost savings. AI is not only innovative; it delivers benefits which are incremental to what is achieved from current transformation programs.

For example, there is a clear use case for AI in category management. Data collection and category research are historically done manually. Strong market knowledge is needed, and stakeholder engagement is required. Fortunately, there is a better, faster way with AI in category management. It normally takes many weeks to manually create a strategy for a new purchasing category, but with AI embedded in category management, research on category specific information is available with a few clicks. Here SAP’s co-pilot, Joule, leverages information from the category initiative and uses historic sourcing data, including commodity code, sourcing event type and baseline spend to create the RFP. It also uses AI to recommend suppliers who meet the business criteria, like low carbon footprint and cost-saving considerations, whilst also including suppliers an organisation may have successfully worked with in the past. What was once days and weeks’ worth of work, can now be completed in a fraction of the time.

As demonstrated above, organisations can operate smarter, respond faster and free up capacity to work on the most value-adding strategic tasks.  What is needed is a clear roadmap for AI to build on, and align with, the overall digitalisation strategy for Procurement. Also, there needs to be validated and compelling business case. This is where the true value of AI becomes a practical reality for CPOs.

Autonomous Spend Management

There is no doubt that AI matters in the world of Procurement. At Sapphire Orlando 2026, SAP unveiled the groundbreaking Autonomous Spend Management. This is a core pillar of the Autonomous Enterprise vision, which uses agentic AI to deliver continuity across procurement, travel, expenses, and external workforce processes. A new suite of Joule Assistants will support the full spend lifecycle, from category management and sourcing to invoicing and expense reporting.

Before we go any further, let us address the “elephant in the room”: AI does not in itself replace people. It amplifies human capability and does not replace human purpose. As with any transformation program, it is for the organisation to decide how it takes the benefits to the bottom line. Also, AI does not replace software. With Autonomous Spend, AI enhances and extends the capabilities of source-to- pay applications with agentic applications which are powered by specialised AI agents. Here, AI assistants and agents, with human oversight, manage and execute the end-to-end processes across source-to-pay. 

Digitalisation is closing the maturity gap, and AI is connecting digitalisation with autonomy. As an organisation matures along its transformation journey, the focus is set to move from basic digitalisation to true autonomy. This is shown in the following diagram.

Autonomous Spend Management builds upon the investment customers have already made in the earlier stages of their transformation. When AI engages with reliability, responsibility and relevance, then we expand the potential of Procurement far more than we diminish it. However, underlying source-to-pay processes cannot still be fragmented if you want to scale the value of AI and realise its true ROI.

 

Conclusion

AI in Procurement will continue to be the “great conversation”. If you have digitised your Procurement and have the foundations in place for further improvement and innovation, then AI will drive further benefits from your procurement transformation program:

 

  • Autonomous Spend Management builds upon the investment customers have already made in the earlier stages of their transformation.
  • AI enhances and extends the capabilities of source-to-pay applications with agentic applications which are powered by specialised AI agents.
  • AI does not in itself replace people, and AI does not replace software. SAP-built AI assistants and agents with human interaction is where the magic really happens for Procurement.

 

 

For further information

  • You can learn more about how AI is transforming Procurement by visiting: SAP Business AI for Procurement
  • See the blog from Etosha Thurman, Chief Marketing Officer for SAP Finance & Spend Management: Enabling Autonomous Spend Management with AI and Connected Processes

 

 

 

Frank Omare

Global Value Advisory, Spend Management

entry.title: Using AI Copilots with Jira to Improve SAP S/4HANA Project Governance
entry.content:
Using AI Copilots with Jira to Improve SAP S/4HANA Project Governance

Introduction

SAP S/4HANA transformation programs generate a huge amount of information every day. Requirements, user stories, defects, testing updates, change requests, risks, dependencies, and action items all move across different teams and workstreams. As programs grow in size and complexity, it becomes increasingly difficult to maintain visibility into what is happening and, more importantly, what requires immediate attention.

In many SAP transformation programs, Jira has become the primary platform for managing delivery activities. Project managers, functional consultants, developers, testers, and business stakeholders all rely on Jira to track progress and coordinate work. While Jira provides strong visibility into program execution, large-scale SAP programs can quickly generate thousands of tickets, hundreds of defects, and multiple dependencies across regions and teams.

At that point, having access to information is no longer the challenge. Understanding what the information is telling you becomes the real challenge.

AI Copilot Integration with Jira

Illustrative example showing how AI copilots can transform Jira delivery data into actionable insights, executive reporting, risk analysis, and governance recommendations for SAP S/4HANA transformation programs.

This is where AI copilots can make a meaningful difference.

By combining Jira data with AI-driven insights, SAP teams can improve governance, identify risks earlier, reduce manual reporting effort, and make faster decisions based on real program trends.

Why Jira Matters in SAP Programs

Traditional SAP projects often relied on spreadsheets, status reports, email updates, and disconnected tracking tools. This made it difficult to maintain a consistent view of project health. Different teams frequently worked with different versions of the truth, and leadership often had to wait until governance meetings to understand the actual status of the program.

Jira helped solve many of these challenges by providing a centralized platform for managing delivery activities.

Today, many SAP S/4HANA programs use Jira to manage:

Jira in an SAP S/4HANA Transformation Program

Illustrative example showing how Jira supports end-to-end delivery management across SAP S/4HANA transformation programs, from business requirements and user stories through testing, cutover readiness, and go-live.

  • Business requirements

  • User stories

  • Configuration activities

  • Development tasks

  • SIT defects

  • UAT defects

  • Change requests

  • Sprint planning

  • Risks and dependencies

  • Cutover and go-live activities

When delivery information is consolidated into a single platform, organizations gain better visibility and coordination across workstreams.

However, visibility alone does not automatically lead to better governance.

The Real Problem with Scale

In smaller projects, Jira is usually easy to manage. In large SAP S/4HANA transformation programs, the situation becomes very different.

Programs may involve:

  • Thousands of Jira tickets

  • Hundreds of open defects

  • Multiple agile teams

  • Global delivery models

  • Several testing cycles

  • Complex integration dependencies

Project managers often spend significant time reviewing dashboards, preparing reports, consolidating updates, and identifying trends across workstreams.

Even with all that effort, important risks can still go unnoticed.

A workstream may appear busy but still be falling behind schedule. Defect volumes may seem stable until they begin affecting testing timelines. A growing backlog may not appear critical until it starts impacting go-live readiness.

This is exactly the type of challenge where AI copilots can help.

How AI Copilots Help

An AI copilot can analyze large volumes of Jira data significantly faster than manual review processes.

More importantly, it can help interpret the data instead of simply displaying it.

For example, an AI copilot can:

  • Detect sprint delivery risks

  • Identify recurring defect patterns

  • Highlight delayed workstreams

  • Predict testing bottlenecks

  • Recommend mitigation actions

  • Generate executive summaries

  • Identify potential resource constraints

Instead of spending hours reviewing dashboards and reports, teams can focus on resolving issues and making informed decisions.

That shift is important because effective governance is not about reporting activity. It is about identifying the right signals early enough to influence outcomes.

Moving from Reactive to Predictive Governance

Many SAP transformation programs still operate reactively.

Teams often identify problems only after milestones slip, testing delays occur, or defect volumes become visible to leadership. By then, remediation becomes more difficult and more expensive.

AI copilots create an opportunity to move from reactive governance to predictive governance.

AI-Driven SAP Project Risk Monitoring

Illustrative example showing how AI can analyze Jira data, defect trends, sprint performance, testing outcomes, dependencies, and resource constraints to identify emerging delivery risks and recommend proactive mitigation actions.

By analyzing information such as:

  • Defect trends

  • Story completion rates

  • Sprint velocity

  • Testing outcomes

  • Dependency relationships

  • Resource allocation patterns

AI can identify signals that suggest future delivery risks.

For example:

  • Repeated sprint misses may indicate capacity constraints.

  • Rising defect trends may signal declining quality.

  • Growing backlogs may suggest future schedule pressure.

  • Delayed dependencies may increase cutover risk.

These patterns often emerge before traditional status reporting identifies a problem.

That early visibility allows leadership teams to act before issues become major program risks.

Better Executive Visibility

One of the most valuable uses of AI copilots is improving executive visibility.

Copilot-Assisted Executive Governance Dashboard

Illustrative executive dashboard showing how AI-generated insights can provide real-time visibility into program health, testing readiness, delivery risks, cutover preparedness, and recommended actions across SAP S/4HANA transformation programs.

Leadership teams rarely need hundreds of Jira tickets or detailed technical reports.

Instead, they need clear answers to questions such as:

  • Are we still on track for go-live?

  • Which workstreams are at risk?

  • What is causing testing delays?

  • Where should leadership focus attention?

  • What mitigation actions are required?

AI copilots can convert large volumes of project data into concise, actionable insights.

This reduces reporting effort while making governance discussions more productive.

Instead of spending time collecting updates, teams can spend more time discussing decisions and actions.

Readiness Monitoring Before Go-Live

As SAP programs approach go-live, readiness becomes increasingly important.

This is another area where AI copilots can provide significant value.

By analyzing information across multiple Jira workstreams, AI can help identify:

  • Open critical defects

  • Incomplete testing activities

  • Cutover preparation gaps

  • Missing business sign-offs

  • Integration readiness concerns

  • Training completion risks

This creates a more dynamic view of transformation readiness compared to traditional manually collected status reports.

When organizations are approaching a go/no-go decision, that visibility becomes extremely valuable.

Governance Still Needs People

Despite the benefits of AI, governance should never become fully automated.

SAP transformations still require project managers, program leaders, functional experts, architects, and business stakeholders to provide judgment, context, and experience.

AI can identify patterns, summarize risks, and recommend actions.

People still make the decisions.

The strongest governance models combine:

  • Reliable project data

  • Strong governance processes

  • Human expertise

  • AI-driven insights

When these elements work together, organizations gain better visibility, faster decision-making, and stronger program control.

Final Thoughts

SAP S/4HANA transformation programs are becoming increasingly complex, and the volume of project information continues to grow.

Jira already provides a strong foundation for managing delivery activities. When AI copilots are layered on top of that foundation, organizations gain the ability to move beyond tracking work and begin understanding what the data is actually telling them.

From my experience, the greatest value of AI in project governance is not automation.

It is visibility.

The earlier teams can identify emerging risks, understand delivery trends, and focus attention on the right priorities, the greater the likelihood of achieving a successful SAP transformation outcome.

In large SAP programs, better visibility often leads to better decisions. And better decisions are ultimately what drive successful transformation outcomes.

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Machine Learning with SAP HANA Cloud and BAS
entry.content:
Machine Learning on structured data might not be what many people have in mind when talking about AI. Yet, it is a form of AI and the massive interest in Large Language Models, agents and assistants is also leading to many new Machine Learning Projects. 

With this blog you can get hands-on with Machine Learning by implementing a time-series forecast in SAP HANA Cloud. In the requirements and projects that I am seeing, time-series forecasting is by far the most popular Machine Learning approach in a SAP related project. From Accounts Receivable forecasting, to demand forecasting and even solar power prediction, a time-series forecast might get you there.

IMPORTANT: You can download all code from this repository.

 

Architecture

The architecture for this example is as straightforward as it can be. Your data is in SAP HANA Cloud, where we also have Machine Learning algorithms (Predictive Analysis Library and Automated Predictive Library). We then use Python in a Jupyter Notebook running in SAP Business Application Studio to trigger the Machine Learning algorithms in SAP HANA Cloud. This is possible with SAP's hana_ml Python package. You can also choose any other Python environment of your choice to use that package to trigger the built-in algorithms. No data ever has to leave SAP HANA Cloud, since data and ML algorithm are already in the same place.

This blog focuses mostly on a stand-alone SAP HANA Cloud. However, you can also trigger the same Machine Learning in SAP Datasphere's embedded SAP HANA Cloud. This blog has more details. 

More components are typically needed for a productive environment. To automate the creation of predictions the Python code can be schedules for instance with Cloud Foundry or SAP AI Core. And the time-series algorithm we are using is just one of 100+ algorithms for you to choose from. Here is an overview. See the hana_ml documentation for details.

 

Prerequisites

This blog assumes that:

  • You have SAP Business Application Studio up and running and configured as described in "Setting up Python in SAP Business Application Studio to trigger HANA Cloud Machine Learning"
  • You have access to a SAP HANA Cloud instance, that is configured as described in the same above blog.

 

Data Load

You will use historic data from the Swiss tourism area to predict how many nights people will spend in a hotel each month in the next 12 months. Download the file HOTELNIGHTS.csv and import it into SAP HANA Cloud, for instance with the SAP HANA Database Explorer. Select "Import Data".

 

Click through the options:

  • name the new table: HOTELNIGHTS
  • set the following column data types
    • MONTH: Date
    • ARRIVALS: Integer
    • HOTELNIGHTS: Integer

 

You will see that the information in that table is quite detailed. It shows for the period from January 2020 to March 2026 by month how many people from which Country spent how many nights in a hotel in the various regions of Switzerland.

 

Time-series forecast

Now use the data for a time-series forecast! Script in Python and the hana_ml package translates your logic into SQL and has SAP HANA Cloud do the work. 

IMPORTANT: You can download the notebook HANAML time series forecast.ipynb, which has the following code included. If you upload that file into your environment and execute the first cell, you will be prompted to select the Python environment. This can be easily overlooked...

If you are meeting the prerequisites (see above), you should already be able to connect form a Jupyter notebook in BAS to connect to your SAP HANA Cloud. Continue now by storing the database user's details a in a credentials file.

{
  "SAP_HANA_CLOUD": {
    "HANA_ADDRESS": "YOURENDPOINT",
    "HANA_PORT": 443,
    "HANA_USER": "AIUSER",
    "HANA_PASSWORD": "YOURSECRETPASSWORD"
  }
}

 

Load these credentials in Python and verify that the connection still returns True.

import json
with open('./credentials.json', 'r') as creds:
  credentials = json.load(creds)
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_ADDRESS  = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_ADDRESS"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PORT     = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_PORT"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_USER     = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_USER"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PASSWORD = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_PASSWORD"]
import hana_ml.dataframe as dataframe
conn = dataframe.ConnectionContext(address  = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_ADDRESS,
                                   port     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PORT,
                                   user     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_USER,
                                   password = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PASSWORD)
conn.connection.isconnected()

In case you see a compatibility warning for Python 3.0.2, feel free to ignore it.

Continue by creating a hana_ml DataFrame, which points to the Data in SAP HANA Cloud. The data remains in SAP HANA Cloud. No records have yet left the system. This is by default and on purpose. The intention is to keep the data in the system and do any data processing within SAP HANA Cloud.

data_hdf = conn.table('HOTELNIGHTS')

 

It is possible to retrieve data though. You just need to request it with the collect()-method, which downloads the data of the hana_ml DataFrame as Pandas DataFrame. To avoid downloading all records, first restrict the data to 5 rows with the head()-method. The sequence is very important here. First reduce the number of records, then download.

data_hdf.head(5).collect()

 

To get a more detailed feel for the data, have some statistics calculated.

data_hdf.describe().collect()

 

There are 13 Swiss regions in the data for example and the maximum number of hotel nights in a specific months in a specific region from a specific country is 223091. Important here is the fact that every value in that table was calculated in SAP HANA Cloud. Imagine you are working with a massive table, you will want to leverage the processing power of SAP HANA Cloud. That's ensured here, since the hana_ml package created the necessary SQL statement. That SQL syntax can be seen by adding ".select_statement" to the command. The screenshot shows only a fraction of the SQL.

import pprint
pprint.pp(data_hdf.describe().select_statement)

 

One of many other ways to explore the data is an interactive standard report.

from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(data_hdf).build().display()

 

We are happy with the data and need to prepare it now for a monthly forecast of all hotel nights in the country. Aggregate by month. 

agg_hdf = data_hdf.agg([('sum', 'HOTELNIGHTS', 'HOTELNIGHTS')], group_by='MONTH')
agg_hdf = agg_hdf.sort('MONTH')
agg_hdf.head(5).collect()

As usual, that aggregation is also done on SAP HANA Cloud and only 5 aggregated values are downloaded. Also as usual, you could add ".select_statement" to see the exact SQL syntax that is used.

Plot the monthly data to get a visual impression for the data. This requires the installation of another Python package. Run this cell once. After the package is installed:

  • you may want to delete the cell (or comment it out) to avoid unnecessary reinstallation attempts)
  • and you may need to restart the Python kernel so that the new package can be used. Click the "Restart" icon in the menu on top and run the earlier cells again.
!pip install nbformat # Once installed, turn this line into a comment to avoid running it again

 

Download the monthly aggregates and create a line chart.

import plotly.express as px
data_df = agg_hdf.collect()
fig =  px.line(data_df, x='MONTH', y='HOTELNIGHTS', title='Hotel nights by Month')
fig.show()

There is a beautiful repeating pattern in the data. True seasonality. Peaks in the summer, lows in November and lower peaks in winter. Train a time-series model! We use the AdditiveModelForecast which is an implementation of Meta's Prophet algorithm.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.tsa.additive_model_forecast import AdditiveModelForecast
amf = AdditiveModelForecast()
amf.fit(data=agg_hdf)

 

Explore the trained model in another interactive standard report.

from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(amf).build().display()

 

With that trained model we can forecast the unknown future. Just prepare a hana_ml DataFrame that contains the months (or dates), for which we want forecasts. In a productive scenario you will probably connect to a table that's permanently updated with new records. Hence, to keep the code dynamic for scheduling, find out from the data the most recent month for which data is available. 

str_lastdate = agg_hdf.tail(1, ref_col='MONTH').collect().iloc[0,0]
str_lastdate = str(str_lastdate)[0:10]
print(str_lastdate)

 

Use that month as basis create the required dates for the forecast. We need 12 records, always the first day of the following 12 months.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.random import binomial
months_to_forecast=12
future_hdf = binomial(conn, n=1, p=1, num_random=months_to_forecast)
future_hdf = future_hdf.select('*', (f'''ADD_MONTHS(TO_DATE ('{str_lastdate}', 'YYYY-MM-DD'), ID+1)''', 'MONTH') )
future_hdf = future_hdf.select('MONTH', ('0', 'TARGET'))
future_hdf.head(20).collect()

 

Apply the trained model to predict those dates. And you get the prediction (YHAT) as well as the prediction interval (YHAT_LOWER and YHAT_UPPER).

predicted_hdf = amf.predict(data=future_hdf)
predicted_hdf.head(20).collect()

Before using the predictions, have a closer look at the trained model and the forecasted values in the standard report.

 

The outlier tab points out a few months in 2020 that are unusual. Indeed, there was something that year that impacted travelling and hotel nights....  Cutting off that old history would be a good idea. You could go back to the top of the Notebook and add a filter to the data, ie 

data_hdf = data_hdf.filter("YEAR(MONTH) >= 2022")

 

In this tutorial we continue with the model as it is. You could save the predictions just as they are. However, you may also want to combine the past actuals with the predictions into a single table since that might make it easier to put a dashboard on top for instance.

predicted_hdf = predicted_hdf.select('MONTH',
                                    ('NULL', 'HOTELNIGHTS'),
                                    ('YHAT', 'FORECAST'),
                                    ('YHAT_LOWER', 'FORECAST_LOWER'),
                                    ('YHAT_UPPER', 'FORECAST_UPPER'))
agg_hdf = agg_hdf.select('*', 
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST'),
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST_LOWER'),
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST_UPPER'))
actualsandpred_hdf = predicted_hdf.union(agg_hdf)
actualsandpred_hdf.sort('MONTH').tail(5).collect()

 

Finally, save that combined data into a table in SAP HANA Cloud. In case that table doesn't exist yet, it will be automatically created in the format of the hana_ml DataFrame.

actualsandpred_hdf.save('HOTELNIGHTS_FORECAST', force=True)

 

With this you have enriched your data in SAP HANA Cloud through Machine Learning! The actuals in the HOTELNIGHTS column end in March 2026 with the FORECAST continuing on from April 2026 to March 2027.

 

Related topics

With such a forecast you can already achieve a lot. You may also want to extend it further or use a completely different algorithm. These resources can help you along, but feel free to ask if you have any questions!

  • Documentation hana_ml package
  • Documentation of the underlying Predictive Analysis Library
  • Sample code for the Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) and also the Automated Predictive Library (APL)

entry.title: From Agent to MCP: What Building an MCP Taught Me About the Future of AI Integration
entry.content:
1. The moment that sparked the question

I've been building automations for a few years now. Not because someone asked me to — because I genuinely wanted to see if it worked.

It started with RPA. And honestly, for what it was, it worked. It solved real problems. But every time a process changed, the bot broke. Every time something shifted upstream, I was back in the designer fixing selectors and re-mapping fields. It was useful — just fragile in a way that bothered me.

Then I added LLMs to the mix. Suddenly the automation could handle variability, could read a document instead of just clicking through a form. That was exciting. But now I had two systems with completely different failure modes sitting next to each other, and I was the glue.

So I went further. I built a custom agent from scratch — a proper one, with skills, context management, decision logic. I wrote about it here. That project taught me more about how AI systems actually behave than anything I'd read. And when it worked, it really worked.

But there's always a next thing to build.

When Joule Work Desktop was announced at Sapphire, MCP was the integration standard. I didn't overthink it — if I wanted to connect to Joule, that was the path. What I didn't expect was how much building an MCP would make me question everything I thought I already understood.

The surface looks familiar: you're still giving a model tools to call, still thinking about context, still asking yourself what the model needs to know to be useful. But the architecture underneath is different in ways that matter. And those differences aren't obvious until you're in the middle of building.

This post is about what I found there.

2. Two ways to give an LLM context: Agents with Skills vs. MCP

Both approaches solve the same fundamental problem: the model doesn't know anything about your system unless you tell it.

You define what tools exist. You describe what they do. The model decides when and how to use them. That mechanic is identical in both. If you've built one, the mental model for the other isn't foreign. You're not starting from zero.

That's where the similarity ends — and where it gets interesting.

What surprised me about the agent

I'll be honest: I went into the agent build fully expecting to suffer.

Years of writing integrations the hard way had trained me well. I had my mental checklist ready: parse the API response, map the fields, pass the values explicitly between steps, handle the edge cases nobody documented. I was prepared for a week of plumbing before I could write a single line of actual logic.

Then I typed "for each result, do this" — and the agent just... did it. A proper for-each. No code. No explicit loop. I called an API and the response landed in context already mapped, already interpretable, ready to use in the next step. The model looked at the shape of the data and figured it out.

I sat there for a second genuinely unsure if it had worked or if I was about to discover a very elegant failure.

It had worked.

That moment reset a lot of assumptions I didn't know I was carrying. The plumbing I had prepared to build never needed to exist. What was left was only the part that actually required my brain: the business rules, the scoring logic, the decision criteria. The stuff that actually matters.

What's different about MCP

With the custom agent, I was still the director. The agent had skills, the skills called APIs, I defined the flow, I could see everything happening. It was my system behaving the way I told it to.

MCP is a different mental model — and it took me a moment to fully land on it.

You build a server. You expose tools. And then you let go.

The client — in this case Joule Desktop — connects to your server, discovers what you've built, and decides how to use it based on what the user asks. You don't orchestrate the flow. You don't manage the context. You define the capability and the client figures out the rest.

The first time I really internalized that, my instinct was mild panic. What do you mean I don't control the flow? But then something clicked: I wasn't building an agent anymore. I was building an interface. A set of capabilities that any MCP-compatible client could plug into — today Joule, tomorrow something else entirely. That's not losing control. That's actually a better kind of architecture.

The real difference: who's in charge

  Custom Agent (Skills) MCP

Who orchestratesThe agentThe client (Joule, Claude, etc.)
Where context livesInside the agentInside the client
Who can use the toolsOnly that agentAny MCP-compatible client
DeploymentPlatform-boundIndependent server
What you controlEverythingThe tools and their outputs

Neither is better. The agent gave me more control and visibility — which was exactly right for a workflow that needed precise, predictable steps. MCP gave me portability and decoupling — which was exactly right for something I wanted any compatible client to use without rebuilding from scratch.

The question isn't which one is superior. The question is what you're actually trying to build.

 

3. What surprised me while building

The design changed three times. And that was the real lesson.

When I start a new server, my brain goes straight to the same place it always does: define the endpoints, figure out the data model, write the code. I've done it enough times that it's almost automatic.

That instinct completely failed me here.

The first version had too many tools, each doing too little. Classic API thinking — small, focused, composable. The kind of thing that looks great in a diagram and makes you feel like a responsible adult. The model looked at my beautiful diagram and chose the wrong tool anyway. Every time. It was combining things I hadn't anticipated, skipping steps that seemed obvious, and generally behaving like it had never seen my very clean diagram. Because it hadn't. Because it doesn't work that way.

I went back in. Second version: fewer tools, each doing more. The model seemed happier. I was not. I now had tools that behaved differently depending on the input, and I couldn't clearly explain what they did even to myself. That's usually a sign something has gone wrong.

Third version — something finally clicked. And what changed wasn't the code. It was how I was thinking about the whole thing.

You're not designing endpoints for a developer to call. You're designing capabilities for a model to reason about. The name you give a tool shapes when the model reaches for it. The description isn't documentation — it's an instruction. You're writing for a different kind of reader, and once that landed for me, the design stopped fighting back.

A rule I wish someone had told me on day one: don't start building a tool until you can describe it in one sentence to a non-developer. If you can't explain it clearly, the model won't figure it out either. Write the descriptions before you write the code. Yes, before.

Then I ran into the actual hard part. And it wasn't the server.

The server was running. The tools were working. I was feeling pretty good about myself.

And then someone asked a real question and the answer came back technically correct, well-structured, and completely useless.

That's the failure mode nobody puts in the tutorial. A model without the right context doesn't throw an error — it generates a confident, beautifully formatted response that says absolutely nothing of value. It sounds like it knows what it's talking about. It doesn't. And that's scarier than a stack trace because at least a stack trace tells you something is broken.

I ended up spending more time going back to fix the knowledge layer than I'd spent building the server in the first place. Cleaning structure, rethinking what actually needed to be there versus what was just noise dressed up as content. All work that would have been faster — and less humbling — if I'd treated it as the first problem to solve instead of the thing I'd get to eventually.

The lesson, painfully earned: LLMs don't create knowledge. They surface it. Put noise in, get noise back — in a very professional tone. The knowledge layer is the foundation. The server is just the delivery mechanism. I had those two things backwards for longer than I'd like to admit.

The part I didn't expect to enjoy: vector search

I'll be honest — before this project, "embeddings" was one of those words I nodded at confidently without fully understanding.

The vector search itself went smoother than expected. Once it was running it felt almost like cheating — you send a plain question in natural language and the right content comes back ranked by actual meaning, not keyword overlap. No brittle query strings. No AND/OR logic. Just... the right stuff, most of the time.

But getting there sent me down a rabbit hole I had not budgeted for. What embeddings actually are. Why the model you use to generate them changes what comes back. Why chunking strategy — how you split and index the source content — matters way more than it sounds like it should. Why two distance metrics that seem equivalent can return completely different results.

I didn't plan for any of that. I came out of it genuinely glad I went in — and slightly annoyed that nobody had handed me a map.

4. MCP on SAP BTP: the trust boundary as a feature

There's a version of this that lives somewhere else. A different cloud provider, external APIs for embeddings, a third-party vector database. It would have worked technically.

I didn't build that version.

The decision to run the MCP on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry wasn't just about where to host something. It was about where the knowledge base lives — and what that means when you're building inside an enterprise ecosystem.

The institutional knowledge powering the server comes from SAP sources. Running on BTP meant that authentication, connectivity, and data access were all handled through mechanisms that already existed and were already trusted. No external service credentials to manage. No custom connectors to build. No explaining to anyone why sensitive knowledge was touching a third-party API.

That last one matters more than it sounds.

In enterprise settings, the conversation about data governance can easily become longer than the conversation about the actual product. Running inside the platform's trust boundary meant that conversation was mostly resolved before it started. The data doesn't leave the ecosystem. Embeddings are generated through services already inside it. The vector search runs natively. The MCP sits on Cloud Foundry. It's the same platform all the way through — and in this context, that's a feature, not a constraint.

Using the same platform as both a data store and a vector engine was the piece that made the architecture feel coherent. I wasn't stitching together the best individual tool for each layer and hoping they'd talk to each other cleanly. One platform, one trust model, one less thing to explain to anyone who asked.

The boundary wasn't something I worked around. It was something I designed with.

5. Under the hood

I want to keep this section honest and specific. Not a sales pitch for any particular stack — just the actual pieces, how they connect, and the decisions that shaped them. 

Design

The server itself

The MCP is built in Python using FastMCP — the high-level wrapper from the official MCP SDK. If you've never used it, the experience is surprisingly close to writing a FastAPI app: you define functions, decorate them with @McP.tool(), write a proper docstring, and that's your tool. The docstring isn't optional ceremony — it's what the model reads to decide when and how to call the function. Treat it like an API contract written for a reader who can't ask clarifying questions.

@mcp.tool()
def retrieve_similar_cases(query: str, category: str, top_k: int = 3) -> str:
    """
    Retrieve similar historical cases from the vector store.
    Returns expert-validated signals: category, effort, timeline, impact.
    Use these as directional hints — NOT as the final answer.
    """
    ...

The vector search

This is where the retrieval happens. The core is a HANA Cloud query using COSINE_SIMILARITY against a REAL_VECTOR column — filtered by a dimension, ordered by semantic proximity:

SELECT TOP 3 *
FROM KNOWLEDGE_TABLE
WHERE CATEGORY = ?
ORDER BY COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) DESC

Before this query runs, the incoming text gets converted into an embedding vector via SAP AI Core. That vector is what gets compared against the stored embeddings in HANA. The model that generates the embeddings matters — consistency between ingestion time and query time is non-negotiable. If you embed your knowledge base with model A and query with model B, the cosine similarities will be meaningless. Same model, both directions, always.

For batch processing, the searches run in parallel using ThreadPoolExecutor — one connection per thread, all results collected before returning. The difference in latency between sequential and parallel retrieval across a large batch is significant enough that this wasn't optional.

Two vector indexes, one query pattern

The knowledge base has two separate tables, each with its own EMBEDDING column and its own filter logic. Both are queried using the same cosine similarity pattern, just with different filters and column mappings. The retrieval function runs both searches concurrently and returns a unified result keyed by source type:

with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=len(source_types)) as pool:
    results = dict(pool.map(_search_one, source_types))

Simple, but effective. The model gets both result sets in a single tool call and uses them independently when synthesizing the response.

The transport layer on BTP

On Cloud Foundry, the server runs as a standard Python app bound to a port via the $PORT environment variable. No custom buildpack, no containers — just a Procfile, a manifest.yml, and the app itself. The MCP endpoint is exposed over HTTPS at the app's route, which is what Joule Desktop connects to. Authentication and routing are handled by BTP's built-in infrastructure.

The only thing worth calling out: stateless_http=True in the FastMCP init. MCP sessions over HTTP are stateless by design here — each request carries its own context. That's the right model for a server that multiple clients might connect to simultaneously, and it's what made the BTP deployment straightforward without needing session affinity or sticky routing.

6. Where MCP fits in the SAP AI ecosystem

I want to be careful here not to oversell something I'm still learning. But I also think there's something real happening and it's worth saying out loud.

MCP is gaining momentum inside the SAP ecosystem faster than most integration standards do. And I think the reason is simpler than it seems: it solves a problem that everyone building with AI on SAP has been quietly struggling with — how do you give a model structured access to enterprise capabilities without building a custom integration every single time?

Skills were a good answer. They worked. But they were tightly coupled to a specific agent, on a specific platform, for a specific use case. Every new scenario meant starting over.

MCP decouples that. You build the capability once. Any compatible client can use it. Today that's Joule Desktop. Tomorrow it'll be whatever comes after Joule Desktop, and the MCP you built won't care. That's the kind of architectural bet that tends to age well.

Joule Work Desktop is already in pilot. MCP is the integration path they chose. That's not a coincidence — that's a direction. And the developers who understand how to build MCPs well right now, before it's mainstream, before there are established patterns and ready-made templates for everything, are going to have a significant head start on everyone who waits until it's the obvious thing to do.

I'm not saying drop everything and rewrite your agents as MCPs. I'm saying: if you've been curious, now is a genuinely good time to build something small and see what you learn. The protocol is approachable. The tooling is improving fast. And the mental shift it forces — from thinking about functions to thinking about capabilities a model can reason about — is one of those shifts that changes how you see the whole problem space, not just this one project.

Build something. Even if it's small. Especially if it's small.

The best way to understand what MCP actually changes is to be in the middle of building with it and suddenly realize your old instincts aren't working anymore. That moment of friction is where the learning is. Don't skip it by reading about it — go find it.

entry.title: What We Learned Hosting a SAP Document AI Workshop
entry.content:
Introduction

Some weeks ago we had a hands-on event focused on SAP Document AI and enterprise document automation. We had planned it to be just a technical workshop, yet the attendees wasted no time in telling us all kinds of complications they were experiencing at the time. Soon, we were talking about operational processes, workflow complexity, and the increasing importance of AI in enterprise environments.

When we were planning the workshop we did not just want to show people a product. Our goal was to create a space where participants could engage directly with actual document processing situations, as well as investigate automation options that might enhance their routine workflows. I mean, you can't have an opinion about something you haven't used or experienced yourself.

During the session attendees had the opportunity to experiment with document classification, extraction models, workflow routing and automation processes related to larger SAP workflows.

As the Hackathon went on, the conversations naturally shifted toward some of the operational challenges many companies are currently facing. Although SAP Document AI was the focus, there was also room for repetitive manual work, fragmented validation processes, inconsistent document formats, and the difficulty of scaling document-heavy operations across teams and business units.

 

What the workshop was about

The workshop included product exploration and practical activities, simulating real enterprise scenarios. Rather than merely attend presentations or demos, participants were able to directly engage with workflows, classifiers, extraction schemas, and automation processes.

The event provided participants with insights into how SAP Document AI can support invoicing processes, free-form documents, workflow routing, and document classification.

 

One exercise that participants seemed to find particularly useful was the ROI calculator we brought to the workshop. While most of the day focused on workflows and automation scenarios, the calculator helped ground those discussions in something every organization cares about: business impact. It gave attendees a way to estimate how document automation could affect their own operations and helped move the conversation from technical possibilities to practical outcomes.

I think we were really taken aback by the diversity of perspectives in the room. People came from different industries and business environments, and brought very different operational realities and use cases to the discussion.

We were surprised that many of the issues being discussed were incredibly similar. Within all industries, companies had a lot of the same challenges around managing documents, approvals, integrations and keeping processes consistent. 

While working on practical document-processing scenarios, it became evident that the challenges most companies face go far beyond document extraction. As operations expand across multiple departments and teams, many organizations are still faced with fragmented workflows, repetitive manual validations, inconsistent document formats and processes that are becoming harder to manage.

And the workshop also reinforced something we often see when working with SAP technologies: real conversations happen once people can interact directly with a solution. The hackathon became much more practical and grounded after participants started to set up schemas, test classifiers and experiment with workflows. Rather than considering AI as something far away or theoretical, attendees began to consider how these solutions could realistically be integrated into their own operational environments.

Another important conclusion we arrived at was the importance of integration for successful AI adoption. Precise extraction models are crucial, no one will deny that, but they are just one part of the process. Companies also require workflows capable of adapting to operational changes, handling variations between document types, and collaborating effectively over time with existing systems.

Some Reflections

One of the most gratifying aspects of the workshop was to look back and see how quickly participants started to relate the exercises to their own organizations. Throughout the day the conversation evolved easily from examples under discussion to real business processes, internal priorities and potential next steps. Watching that transition in real time was a reminder of how the value of these sessions often goes far beyond the tech itself.

The workshop was also a great opportunity for us to hear directly from organizations that are facing different operational challenges, and to get a better understanding of how they are approaching document automation and AI adoption within their SAP landscapes.

Some of the most valuable insights came not only from the practical tasks but from the conversations that took place around them. The people who came had different perspectives, experiences and business contexts and we learnt from each other. The workshop was a valuable experience for all involved. As for me, I’m already looking forward to the next one, whether that’s as a host, a participant, or simply someone eager to keep learning.

entry.title: Calling All Applicants for the 2026 Global Delivery Hub Americas High School Summer Orientation!
entry.content:
Each year, SAP’s Global Delivery Hub Americas (formerly Premium Hub CoE NA) hosts high school students to participate in a weeklong Summer Orientation. The 25-hour program provides students with the opportunity to learn about SAP technologies through different activities while receiving educational and career guidance from professionals in the field. SAP experts lead daily, interactive sessions that cover a variety of exciting topics, from coding and data analytics to SAP Cloud, IoT, and Mobile platforms. Over the course of the week, students use their technical skills and newly acquired knowledge to resolve real-world scenarios using SAP technologies. This year we are excited to host the orientation in-person at the Newtown Square campus (outside of Philadelphia). There, students will be able to explore the office, as well as participate in hands-on activities.   

Every year the Summer Orientation features a jam-packed schedule, from expert innovation sessions to career development guidance. The week starts off with a brief introduction of SAP along with a presentation on the various internship opportunities offered to college students. Students also have a career tip session, where they receive professional advice about their educational journey and beyond. Later in the week, students get to meet Balaji Gaddam, SVP Head of Global Delivery Hub Americas. 

Students work on a primary project throughout the week that is always relevant to the latest in technology. The students’ work has evolved over the years, much like how SAP’s products have evolved as well! In previous years of the program, students have built projects using a wide variety of SAP technologies. At the end of the week, students will be able to showcase their project to their fellow peers and Delivery Hub Team members.

High school students interested in attending the 2026 Summer Orientation should fill out the form below and update their most recent transcript. Space is limited for this competitive program and is geared towards rising Juniors and Seniors. Transcripts are required to be considered for the program so they must be uploaded to be considered. No resumes or cover letters are needed. Ideal candidates will have a strong interest in STEM and a desire to pursue further study and/or a career in a STEM-related field.  This is NOT an internship. 

Apply to the STEM Week here with your transcript


Applications will be accepted until Friday, June 12. Students accepted to the program will be notified of the decision by Friday, June 26. The orientation will run in-person at the SAP Newtown Square Campus from July 27 to July 31.  The general hours are 10 am to 3 pm but early dismissal on Friday. Transportation will need to be arranged for the student as we do not provide this. Please note that this is not an internship, it is an unpaid program, and all costs associated with travel will need to be covered by the student. SAP will not cover any costs.

entry.title: Building a Procurement Agent for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition with SAP Joule Studio – Part 2
entry.content:
TL;DR: This is the second post of a three-part series on building a custom AI agent for Joule, specialized in creating purchase requisitions in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition through a single prompt. In this second part, we walk through the steps to build Joule Skills and a technique for item parsing using SAP Build Process Automation. Part 1 can be found here.

 

 

Understanding the Joule Skill

A Joule Skill is a modular capability that SAP Joule can invoke to execute a specific task on your behalf. It is a packaged “action” that defines:

  • Which business intent it addresses (e.g., “get allocation runs”, “list projects”);
  • Which data and systems it needs to call (APIs, services);
  • What the expected inputs and outputs are;
  • And how to execute with proper security and governance.

With Skills, in addition to answering questions, Joule can execute tasks across enterprise systems in a predictable and controlled manner.

 

Typical Structure of a Joule Skill

Here are some common elements in the structure of a Joule Skill built in Joule Studio:

  • Intent and description: the business outcome being delivered.
  • Input parameters: what Joule needs to receive (e.g., period, project ID). Skills primarily work with text (string) values for input parameters.
  • Backend action: integration and queries to APIs of SAP or external systems (through Actions).
  • Output schema: the format of the result to be returned to the user or to the next steps.

 

Joule Skill vs. Joule Agent

Compared to classical developments, Joule Skills can be seen as encapsulated functions with deterministic behavior. What does this mean? Given the same input, the expected outcome of executing a Joule Skill will always be the same.

Unlike Joule Agents, which include a reasoning step, Joule Skills follow a defined logical path, with predictable behavior and consistent results when the same input data is provided. They are tools defined through a low-code platform that will be consumed by AI agents.

Joule Agents, by contrast, are flexible and variable. They run on top of an LLM (Large Language Model) and do not follow a fixed logical path. They use AI reasoning to decide what to do, based on the context available to them. They can plan, reason, and dynamically solve problems using the tools at their disposal. They are defined through the system prompt, which will be covered in more detail in the final part of this series.

 

The Importance of Descriptions

Often, when developing a custom object in the ABAP environment, descriptions are an afterthought. How many times have you come across a structure in the data dictionary with technical field names that could not be inferred, and incomplete or barely meaningful descriptions?

When developing Joule Skills and Agents, this changes: descriptions matter. They help the agent, during the reasoning phase, to better understand what they are working with. As a result, detailed descriptions are highly welcome – both for the people who will maintain your artifacts in the future and to help the Agent achieve a higher success rate during execution.
Important: Use precise, clear English descriptions, since user requests – even when expressed in another language – are translated internally into English.

 

Procurement Scenario Considerations

Now that you understand what differentiates a Joule Agent from a Joule Skill, let’s briefly discuss how our building blocks will relate to each other and the call flow between them. The simplified diagram below illustrates the flow:

Simplified message flow through components

 

 

In the proposed scenario, when sending a single prompt, the business user will already provide the items and their respective fields. The Joule Agent will receive this prompt and, based on its definitions, split the information between the header and items, then invoke our Joule Skill to request the creation of the purchase requisition.

As mentioned, Joule Skill works with input parameters in text format. Inside it, however, the received text must be transformed into a list using an item-parsing technique. There are several ways to do this. In our case, we used a Process built with SAP Build Process Automation.

In this automation, the available script tasks were used to parse the items, to call the Products API, and subsequently to map the values back to the ISO unit of measure required by the Purchase Requisition creation API.

The items, now mapped and enriched with their corresponding units of measure, are returned to the Joule Skill, which is then ready to call our Action to create the purchase requisition. After the SAP S/4HANA execution, the PR object is returned and captured by the Joule Skill. Finally, the flow returns the ID of the generated purchase requisition.

The Joule Agent can then capture this information and present it to the business user as a message.

 

Creating a Process for Item Parsing

Roles: developers.

Although they are not strictly objects created by Joule Studio, both Processes and Automations are part of the SAP Build suite and can be reused inside Joule Skills. This allows previously designed flows and automations to also be integrated with Joule’s extended capabilities.

In our example, we need a Process for one specific reason: the purchase requisition creation Action expects items in a tabular format. Therefore, the string carrying the PR item information must be converted into rows. We will use a script task to demonstrate this case.

For didactic purposes, we will also use the Process to incorporate information from the products API and assign the corresponding ISO unit of measure. This information will also be obtained through a script.

 

To create a Process Automation project, access the SAP Build Lobby. Then, click Create. Select Automated Process and then Process. Add a name and a description for your project, review it, and confirm the creation.

Creating a Process Automation project

 

First, we configure the Trigger. This Process will be invoked from a Joule Skill, so it must work via an API call. Add a name and a description.

Adding a process trigger

 

For the case of Purchase Requisition creation, we will need a data type containing all the fields we expect to send to our Action. To create this structure, return to the newly created project and choose Create > Data Type, then provide the name and the description.

Creating a data type

 

In our use case, we created a flat structure similar to the one in the API. The fields included in the structure were:

Name

Type

List

Required

Plant

String

No

Yes

BaseUnit

String

No

No

Material

String

No

Yes

DeliveryDate

Date

No

Yes

RequestedQuantity

String

No

Yes

PurchaseRequisitionItem

String

No

Yes

PurchaseRequisition

String

No

Yes

ZJoule

String

No

No

Note: Remember that the ZJoule field was enabled through key user extensibility. If you are reproducing this example, keep in mind that this field will not exist unless you enable it beforehand. Execution will not be impacted if you have not done so – simply skip this field in all the steps.

 

Back to the Process, we will define the input/output parameters and the custom variables. Process Inputs and Outputs are the parameters received and returned by the Process. Custom variables are variables that exist only during the execution of the automation.

To access this section, click on an empty area and then on the << button.

Editing Process parameters

 

For our scenario, we defined the parameters below:

Parameter type

Name

Description

Type

Required

List

Process Input

Purchase Requisition Items – In

Purchase requisition items – trigger

String

Yes

No

Process Output

Purchase Requisition Items

Parsed purchase requisition items

Purchase Requisition Item

Yes

Yes

Custom Variable

Purchase requisition items

Purchase requisitions items

Purchase Requisition Item

N/A

Yes

Custom Variable

Product filter

Product filter string

String

N/A

No

A few additional comments:

  • “Purchase Requisition Items – In” is the parameter that contains all the items inferred by the Agent, in string format.
  • “Purchase Requisition Items” is the output parameter that will hold the items in list format. Note that we activated the List property for this parameter.
  • “Purchase requisition items” and “Product filter” are, respectively, the variable in which the items will be processed and enriched, and the variable that holds a filter string in the format expected by the OData service.

 

Now, we will start building the process flow. After the trigger, click the + icon to add a step and select the Script Task option. Here, we set the name (“Map purchase requisition items”) and click the Edit Script button to open the editor. Then we insert the JavaScript code below, which performs the item mapping and builds the product filter:

// Purchase requisition items
// Parsing items (string to JSON)
var lines = JSON.parse($.context.startEvent.purchaseRequisitionItemsIn);
var index = 0;

// Function formatDate – receives a date object and returns a string with format “yyyy-mm-dd”
function formatDate(date) {
    var yyyy = date.getFullYear();
    var mm = String(date.getMonth() + 1);
    var dd = String(date.getDate());
    if (mm.length < 2) mm = '0' + mm;
    if (dd.length < 2) dd = '0' + dd;
    return yyyy + '-' + mm + '-' + dd;
}

// From parsed array, map values to the custom variable purchaseRequisitionItems
$.context.custom.purchaseRequisitionItems = lines.map(function(line) {
    index += 10;

    return {
        PurchaseRequisition: "",
        PurchaseRequisitionItem: index.toString(),
        Material: line.Material,
        Plant: line.Plant,
        DeliveryDate: formatDate(new Date(line.DeliveryDate)),
        RequestedQuantity: parseFloat(line.RequestedQuantity).toString(),
        BaseUnit: line.BaseUnit,
        ZJoule: line.ZJoule
    };
});

// Build filter string and set value to variable productFilter
function buildFilterString(lines) {
      var filter = "Product eq '" + lines[0].Material + "'";
      for (var i = 1; i < lines.length; i++) {
          filter += " or Product eq '" + lines[i].Material + "'";
      }
      return filter;
}

$.context.custom.productFilter = buildFilterString(lines);

 Important: The JavaScript code provided must be compatible with ECMAScript 5.1.

 

Creating a script task

Note: If you are not familiar with creating scripts, refer to the page Create and Configure a Script Task

Next, the API to retrieve product/material details will be called. We pick the Action for products created in part 1. When you select it, a new dependency will be added to your project.

Adding an Action to your Process flow

 

 

You will need to create a destination variable (which will be requested when testing and deploying this Process) and the input parameters. We assigned the variable Product filter as the value mapped to the $filter parameter.

 

Then we add a second script that looks up the result in the product list and maps the ISO unit of measure to the Purchase Requisition Items variable.

  var sourceList = $.context.action_get_Product_1.result.value;
  var targetList = $.context.custom.purchaseRequisitionItems;

  // Build a lookup map from Product -> BaseISOUnit
  var sourceMap = {};
  for (var i = 0; i < sourceList.length; i++) {
      var sourceItem = sourceList[i];
      sourceMap[sourceItem.Product] = sourceItem.BaseISOUnit;
  }

  // Map BaseISOUnit to BaseUnit in the target list
  for (var j = 0; j < targetList.length; j++) {
      var targetItem = targetList[j];
      var material = targetItem.Material;
      if (sourceMap.hasOwnProperty(material)) {
          targetItem.BaseUnit = sourceMap[material];
      }
  }

 

Adding the second script task

 

The last step in building the Process is mapping its output. When you click End, the side menu displays the Process Outputs section. Here, the value of the Purchase Requisition Items variable will be transferred to the result. Since both structures are of the same type, all fields will be automatically filled in.

Mapping the Process output

 

The Process is now ready to be saved, released, and deployed to the Easy Purchase Requisition environment. This sequence can be executed by clicking Save and then Release. A pop-up will open, where you can enter the version details. Once released, you can click the generated version and select Deploy, then choose your environment and the destination used internally by the API (in our example, EPRProductV4).

 

Saving, releasing, and deploying a Process

 

Back at the lobby, you must publish your automation. If you do not, it will not be available to your Joule Skill. To publish, select the Process, navigate to the Versions tab, click the “…” button on your Released version, and choose the option Publish to Library.

Publishing a Process

 

Creating a Joule Skill

Roles: functional consultants and developers.

The item-parsing prerequisites have been met by the automation we just generated. Now we can proceed with the creation of a Joule Skill.

First, we go to the project. From the SAP Build Lobby, choose Create > Joule Agent and Skill.

Creating a Joule Agent and Skill project from SAP Build lobby

 

Provide a name and a description for your project.

Project name and description

If you need additional information on how to proceed with project creation, refer to the official documentation.

 

When the project opens, select Create > Joule Skill.

Creating a Joule Skill

 

In the pop-up, give your new capability a meaningful name and a detailed description of the purpose it covers. Remember: descriptions help the agent identify the most relevant case for a request from your business user. You should include information about the goal and the parameters expected for its execution.

Joule Skill - initial parameters

 

For our use case, we set the name to “Create a Purchase Requisition” and the description to “A skill to create any type of purchase requisition, based on the PR type and items described through a single prompt, including custom fields (e.g. ZJoule)”.

 

Structuring a Joule Skill is very similar to structuring a Process or an Automation in SAP Build. You will have access to a low-code tool to structure the process flow. To learn everything you can do in a Joule Skill, refer to the documentation Create a Joule Skill.

 

First, we need to define the behavior of the Joule Skill. By clicking anywhere on the background and then on the << button, the configurations will be displayed. In the General tab, you will find the name and description proposed earlier. If necessary, you can also correct or enhance them. There are also two switches:

  • Allow Joule to generate a response: when enabled, this option allows Joule to extract information from the skill and turn it into a response. If you decide not to enable it, you will need to configure the “Send Message” steps to deliver the response. For more information on managing responses, refer to “Send a Response”.
  • Allow skill to be started directly by a user: when enabled, this option allows the capability to be invoked directly by the user, i.e., without an agent or another capability acting as an intermediary. When this option is disabled, the Joule Skill must necessarily have at least one output parameter.

General tab - Joule Skill

 

For this use case, we used:

  • Allow Joule to generate a response: enabled. This will allow Joule to capture the capability’s return and compose its own response.
  • Allow skill to be started directly by a user: disabled. Since there is a step that captures and stringifies the items with a specific structure, it is not advisable to allow the user to invoke the skill directly. We will delegate to the Joule Agent the task of formatting the parameters and invoking the capability.

 

On the Parameters tab, just as we did for the Process, we define the input and output parameters and variables.

Setting input and output parameters

 

In our procurement example, the parameters were configured according to the table below:

Parameter type

Name

Description

Type

Required

List

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Items

Purchase requisition items as a stringified array

String

Yes

No

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Type

Purchase requisition type

String

Yes

No

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Description

Purchase requisition description

String

No

No

Skill Input

Purchase Requisition Header Note

Purchase requisition header note

String

No

No

Skill Output

Purchase Requisition ID

Generated purchase requisition ID

String

No

No

Skill Variable

Purchase Requisition ID

Purchase requisition ID

String

N/A

Yes

 

After completing the parameter configuration, we will create the flow to be executed. To do so, click the + symbol and insert the steps, mapping the inputs and outputs of each activity type.

 Adding a Process task to Joule Skill

 

In our example, we started by inserting the Process Easy Purchase Requisition – Item Parsing in order to transform and enrich the items described by the input parameter. We requested that execution be awaited until completion so that the process could return its output, containing a list of items with their respective ISO units of measure mapped.

The table below summarizes the configuration applied to this step:

Adding an Action call

 

Mapping Action parameters

 

Next, we inserted the Action (API) call to create a purchase requisition. Similar to what was done in the Process for the products Action, here we mapped the inputs and outputs. We also defined an environment variable that holds the destination name to be used by the API call.
